title: Chapter 49: Aftermath
content: The rain fell without mercy.

Wren stood in the alley behind Blackmoss, water streaming down her face, and for a long moment she couldn't tell if she was laughing or crying. The broadcast was still running — she could feel it, a hum in the data streams around her, the signal pulsing like a second heartbeat beneath the skin of the city.

Ash slumped against the brick wall beside her. His legs gave out slowly, like a building surrendering to its own weight, and he slid down until he was sitting on the wet concrete with his head tipped back against the wall. Rain hit his upturned face. He didn't flinch.

The suppressant was still working through his system. She could see it in the dullness of his tattoos, the way his glitch-signature kept flickering between visible and gone. His body was fighting the drug, trying to reclaim itself, but the fight was slow and exhausting and he was running on empty.
She sat down next to him.

The alley smelled of rust and wet garbage and ozone from the electrical conduits running overhead. A rat skittered past, uninterested in two people it couldn't quite perceive. The rain drummed on dumpsters and fire escapes, a percussion track for the chaos unfolding inside Blackmoss's walls.

Through the rain, Wren could hear it — the city waking up to what had happened. Distant sirens, converging on the facility. The thwop-thwop of news helicopters cutting through the storm. And beneath it all, the vast digital roar of millions of screens displaying the same impossible truth.

She pulled out a phone she'd lifted from a security station. The screen was alight with notifications. Every feed, every channel, every platform — the subject files were everywhere. People were posting reactions in real time. Confusion. Horror. Disbelief. Someone had already made a montage of the names set to somber music. Someone else was arguing it was fake.
It wasn't fake.
SUBJECT 03 — ALEXANDER CHEN — ERASED.
That name was real. That life was real. The erasure was real.
Wren set the phone down on the wet ground and let the rain claim it.

"You came for me," Ash said. His voice was rough, scraped raw by the suppressant and the shouting and the effort of pushing his glitch past every limit he had. He wasn't looking at her — he was looking at the sky, letting the rain fill his mouth.
"You came into that building," he continued. "Surrounded by Hounds. With a key you didn't know would work. For me."

Wren pulled her knees up to her chest. The cold was settling into her bones, the way it always did after a surge. Her glitch-power left her hollowed out afterward, a vessel that had poured everything it held into the signal and now sat empty.
"I told you," she said. "You don't get to decide what's good for me."

Ash let out a breath that was almost a laugh. Almost. It caught somewhere in his chest and came out as a sound she'd never heard from him before — raw, broken, relieved. He pressed the heels of his hands against his temples.
"I was ready to disappear," he said. "I had it planned. I was going to let them take me back under, let them scrub whatever was left of my signature. I was going to become nothing, Wren. On purpose."
"I know."

"You knew?" He turned his head to look at her. The rain had plastered his hair to his skull, and in the dim light from a distant streetlamp, she could see the faint glow returning to his tattoos — the suppressant losing its hold, his glitch reasserting itself cell by cell.
"I knew you were giving up," she said. "That's why I couldn't wait."

They sat in silence for a while. The rain didn't ease. The helicopters grew louder, then receded, circling toward the facility's main entrance where the press was already gathering. Yellow crime-scene tape visible through the downpour. Flashing lights blue and red painting the wet streets.

Wren could feel the data streams shifting. The broadcast had reached critical mass — it was self-replicating now, cached on servers across the city, mirrored on platforms that NeuroScale had no jurisdiction over. Even if Vance somehow reclaimed control of Blackmoss's systems, the files were already out. Forever.
That word — forever — settled into her like a warm stone.
"You have the Key?" Ash asked.
She touched her jacket. The Signal Key was warm against her ribs, pulsing faintly with residual charge. "I have it."
He nodded. Closed his eyes. The rain ran down his face like tears he would never shed on purpose.

She watched him. In the glitch-space between them, she could see him more clearly than she'd ever seen anyone — not just his shape, not just the glow of his tattoos, but the frequency of him. The resonance that made him Ash. Three years of invisibility had refined her perception in ways she was still discovering, and right now, in this rain-soaked alley with the world collapsing into chaos all around them, she could see him.
Truly see him.
And he could see her.

It shouldn't have been enough. They were soaked and exhausted and hunted. Vance was still inside Blackmoss, and NeuroScale was a wounded animal that would lash out before it died. There were Hounds to evade, identities to protect, a war that had only just begun.
But for this one moment — sitting in the rain with the Signal Key warm against her chest and Ash's shoulder barely touching hers — it was enough.
She reached over and took his hand. His fingers closed around hers, tight, desperate, certain.
"We have to keep moving," she said.
"I know."
Neither of them moved. Not yet.
The broadcast scrolled on. The city was waking up. And somewhere in the data streams, name after name after name found its way into the light.
Just a few more seconds of this. That was all she asked. Just a few more seconds of being seen.
Then they ran.
